notes.note: A hill-town in Picenum that Labienus had founded and fortified at his own cost; the dispatch quoted here reports it held for Pompey at the very start of the war, though Caesar soon took it. Cicero quotes a Pompeian bulletin word for word.
Cicero recoils that Caesar, a Roman commander, is being described as if he were Hannibal marching on Italy: an enemy seizing citizen towns, not a magistrate of the Roman people.

An allusion to the Gallic sack of Rome (traditionally 390 BC), when the Romans abandoned the city but held the Capitol; Cicero invokes it to argue that even then the citadel was kept, against Pompey's wholesale evacuation.

The Athenian who in 480 BC persuaded Athens to abandon the city and meet the Persians at sea (Salamis); Pompey's defenders cite the precedent, but Cicero answers that the cases differ - Athens faced a barbarian flood, Rome does not.

notes.anchor: But Pericles did not do the same
notes.note: In the Peloponnesian War (from 431 BC) Pericles brought the Attic population inside the walls of Athens but did not surrender the city itself; Cicero offers him as the counter-precedent to Themistocles, about fifty years later.

notes.anchor: to whom the levy and the chief matters of business shall be referred
notes.note: Pompey commissioned Cicero to oversee Campania and the seaboard and to conduct the dilectus, the conscription of troops; the role made Cicero a regional supervisor for the Pompeian war effort along the coast where his villas lay.
